content:
I think I found a way to abstract device IDs without any macros. This is the user-side diff of the change (using rnvme as example):

diff --git a/drivers/block/rnvme.rs b/drivers/block/rnvme.rs
index 427d1161388c..96bd96b7070d 100644
--- a/drivers/block/rnvme.rs
+++ b/drivers/block/rnvme.rs
@@ -566,10 +566,13 @@ fn dbbuf_set(
 impl pci::Driver for NvmeDevice {
     type Data = Arc<NvmeData>;

-    define_pci_id_table! {
-        (),
-        [ (pci::DeviceId::with_class(bindings::PCI_CLASS_STORAGE_EXPRESS, 0xffffff), None) ]
-    }
+    type IdInfo = ();
+
+    const ID_TABLE: &'static dyn kernel::device_id::IdTable<pci::DeviceId, ()> =
+        &kernel::device_id::IdArray::new([(
+            pci::DeviceId::with_class(bindings::PCI_CLASS_STORAGE_EXPRESS, 0xffffff),
+            (),
+        )]);

     fn probe(pci_dev: &mut pci::Device, _id: Option<&Self::IdInfo>) -> Result<Arc<NvmeData>> {
         pr_info!("probe called!\n");

Still hacking around, will share my branch as soon as I get a MVP
Cool!
(deleted)
Did you change C to remove the sentinel?
No, sentinel is still added by IdTable::new
https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/tree/rnvme-device-id-without-macro

Two key tricks here:

  1. Use MaybeUninit and transmute_copy to essentially implement array unzip using const fn. This avoids macros for first/second element.
  2. Use transmute_copy + raw pointer ops to avoid to_rawid function, so it neither needs macro nor const trait impl
That's pretty nice. If you don't mind, I'll pick this up and add you as Co-author and add your signed-off-by.
Feel free
I'd probably add a type alias for &'static dyn kernel::device_id::IdTable<T, Self::IdInfo> Otherwise this looks a bit too verbose I think.
Or you can keep the top-level macro, but expand it to const call directly
Yeah, I was considering that as well.
Not yet sure which one I like better.

type IdInfo = ();

    const ID_TABLE: pci::IdTable<Self::IdInfo> = &IdArray::new([(
        pci::DeviceId::new(bindings::PCI_VENDOR_ID_NVIDIA, bindings::PCI_ANY_ID as u32),
        (),
    )]);

So, that's how it turns out with just a type alias. Pretty clean, I think.
just one question, the macro version permitted None as the value, do we want people to just set IdInfo = Option<...>, or why did the macro version have that?
I think the macro version inherited None from the old const eval version
It's actually quite hard to make the const fn actually work because IdInfo doesn't need to be Copy
But I managed to use MaybeUninit + transmute_copy a lot to work that around
If people actually want IdInfo only sometimes then I think asking them to set IdInfo = Option<...> is a reasonable ask
yeah I also think it's reasonable, was just wondering why the previous version had it automatic

What do we do with

// Stable in Rust 1.79
#![feature(inline_const)]

Can we suppress warnings for later compilers?
It's a lint. You can silence it with -Awhatever-the-lint-is-called
#![allow(stable_features)]
-Astable_features is passed since v6.11, so that one should not be a problem.

I noticed that the changes mentioned here landed into: staging/rust-device. I took the opportunity to rebased my branches on top of it.

I rewrote the modpost alias generation in consequence: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/e457464ffa7e3c2ff94237bca4c263bc60ddd467
Hopefully this wasn't done already by someone else.

In device drivers, the usage ends up like this:

kernel::of_device_table!(
    MODULE_OF_TABLE,
    OF_TABLE,
    [(of::DeviceId::with_compatible(c_str!("onnn,ncv6336")), ()),]
);

...

impl i2c::Driver for Ncv6336 {
    const I2C_DEVICE_ID_TABLE: i2c::IdTable<()> = &I2C_TABLE;
    const OF_DEVICE_ID_TABLE: of::IdTable<()> = &OF_TABLE;

The usage of the macros are not necessary if you don't care about the modpost aliases, but I think in most case people would want to use them for i2c and DT.

Example: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/32db17bfac1270e45e0d45adba19b6b4e96d3cd9#diff-81e1707f4f5073abc61ff6e43d66360a23b3f6ffb078a6c0678e481c97205782R107
@Fabien Parent What I came up with is very close to this. What I'm struggling with (and dislike) is that we need to define the actual ID table outside of the driver scope.
I didn't really see any other options.
Neither do I, unfortunately.
It'd also not resolve with an #[alias] annotation unfortunately, since it wouldn't work with const I guess and static associates are not supported either.

My approach was to make IdTable a struct again and use it to also store the whole size of the array for modpost. And then integrate the modpost macro in module_*_driver!.

This way, we could keep the definition of the ID table within the impl block.
It would make IdTable::new unsafe though.
So, I think your solution sucks less. Mind if I pick it up with some slight modifications and add your CDB and SOB?
Sure

I do hit a compiler issue with your code. It happens when the raw device ID (e.g. struct pci_device_id) in 

static $module_table_name: [u8; $table_name.raw_ids().size()] = unsafe {
    core::mem::transmute_copy($table_name.raw_ids())
};

has padding. The compiler complains about the padding value being uninitialized.

I can reproduce this in a simple example as well: https://godbolt.org/z/M8dzrdjzs

The complaint maybe somehow makes sense I guess, but It only happens in const context. It also goes away when wrapping Foo (from the example) in MaybeUninit, which is a bit weird, because MaybeUninit claims to be transparent.
I had the same issue with I2C. I used core::mem::MaybeUninit::zeroed() to make sure every single byte is initialized.
Transparent only means the layout is identical. u8 cannot hold an uninitialized byte.

Gary Guo said:

Transparent only means the layout is identical. u8 cannot hold an uninitialized byte.

Sure, but I mean MaybeUninit should be transparent, so MaybeUninit<Foo> should have the same layout as Foo. Why is it fine to convert MaybeUninit<Foo> to [u8] in this case?
It's still UB if MaybeUninit<Foo> contains an uninitialized byte
You'll need to transmute it to [MaybeUninit<u8>; ...] it there's an uninitialized byte

It's still UB if MaybeUninit<Foo> contains an uninitialized byte

@Gary Guo That's what I would expect, but the compiler error goes away for some reason.
UB with uninitialized bytes are not always caught. I don't think it's ever caught outside of const context.

Danilo Krummrich said:

It's still UB if MaybeUninit<Foo> contains an uninitialized byte

Gary Guo That's what I would expect, but the compiler error goes away for some reason.

Do you have an example? The minimal example you give doesn't show that when I add MaybeUninit to Foo.
